speaker: interviewee
text: And this is roll number two.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could bring an example to illustrate just what I said a while ago. In September of 1990-- of September, 1942, an order came from the German authorities to the Lodz ghetto that they need, I think, either 5,000 or 10,000 Jews. All those who are over, I think, the age of 45 or 50, all those who are sick, and children up to about 8 or 10 years. I don't remember the details, but more or less this was the request. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Naturally, everybody knew that these people are not going to work. And-- and since we knew from the rumors of the past two years, we realized that these people are going to be killed somewhere, someplace. And the Jews decided not to obey this order. And the Judenrat decided not to do it. And the Germans themselves came in. And they ordered a curfew for eight days. No one was allowed to work. No one was allowed to leave their houses. They came in, and they cordoned off a square block of homes, and they ordered all the people to go-- to come down to the courtyard. They ordered the Jewish police that went with them to go from room to room and look for people that are hiding, if they were hiding. Whoever was hiding was shot on the spot. And those who were still in the rooms will-- had to-- they were moved down to the courtyard. And we were assembled in the courtyard. And the-- the Germans would select small children, elderly, and sickly-looking people. They were moved out from the courtyard into the streets, where trucks were waiting. They were loaded on the trucks and moved to the railroad station. When they got through with this quarter, they told the remaining Jews to go back to their houses and stay there. And they moved to the next block of houses, or to the next street. And this went on, from block to block, for eight days. And instead of 5,000 or 10,000 that they originally asked for, they picked up, if I remember correctly, about 15,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And this was the worst and the biggest [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] evacuation of Jews from the ghetto into-- and at that time they were sent-- we-- we found out later that they were-- they were sent to Auschwitz, to the crematorias.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out about their fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, rumors came in. Because it was-- it was a time that-- you see, what we found out was that when the Jews came into Auschwitz, the clothes were taken off from them, and they were sent to the crematorias. At certain-- at certain times, the clothes were sent back to the Lodz ghetto for cleaning. And people started to recognize certain pieces-- certain articles of clothing. And they knew that something was wrong, because there was no reason for them to-- to get-- to get back or to see these pieces of clothing. So they recognized, and they understood that something happened to them. And then, again, more and more-- when the extermination of the Jews began to be spread out, in the-- in '42 and '43, more and more ideas and more and more news came in, into the ghetto, about things that [INAUDIBLE] there are such things like concentration camps and labor camps and-- and extermination camps. Gas chambers and crematorias and so on. But there was very little we could-- we could do about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to the president of the Judenrat, or the Jewish council, Mr. Rumkowski? What was his fate?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- in the-- in the beginning of August of 1944, the German who was-- the German civilian who was in charge of the Lodz came into the ghetto, and he made speeches that the Russian army is coming closer to the-- to Lodz. And because we were such good workers for the German authorities, and for the German war effort, he wants to save us. And, in order for us to be saved, the Lodz ghetto has to be evacuated-- not liquidated, but evacuated-- deep into Germany where we, because we were so good, we will work for the German authorities. We will continue working under better conditions, under safe conditions. And we should voluntarily go by groups into the trains. And we will go into Germany, into special places where we will continue to work at the same places, in the same trades, in the same services as we did before. Naturally, we did not believe them. And we wouldn't go. So the German soldiers came in and, by force, they started to move us out. And every day-- First of all, we stopped-- we stopped working. And we had to move out from our homes into the plants where we worked. And we stayed there until our next came to be evacuated. Our-- our transport, where I was with my family, left the ghetto on the 24th of August.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. I understand that, two days later, Rumkowski with his family was shipped out from the ghetto into Auschwitz. When our transport came to Auschwitz, the train stopped, the doors opened up-- this is a special chapter, a separate chapter, just to describe it. The conditions in that train, in that cattle-car train-- that it's supposed to-- normally, the trip would-- should take about six, seven hours. It took us about three days to get there. This is a chapter by itself. But when we came, when the doors opened up, we literally fell out from these trains, from these cattle cars, with our bundles-- whatever we took with us. And we were selected-- women separately, men separately. And then each group was, again, selected, between those who were still capable of work went to one place, and those who were not went to a different place. And they were marched off directly to the crematoria. Now in case of Rumkowski, when he came in, eyewitnesses told us, later on, after the liberation, that he was picked out from the trains. He came in the same cattle train, in the same cattle car, together with-- with all others. He was picked out from the train, directly, and moved straight to the crematoria.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: He and his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: He and the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all the-- his work for the Germans didn't save his skin.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it did not save-- no. And the way he acted-- and peop-- eyewitness told us later on that, until the last minute, he still believed that he is still the head of the ghetto. Because when he came down, when he came off the train, he behaved himself like he-- I mean, he beheld-- behaved himself like he would be still the head of a-- of a-- of the Jews or of a Jewish group, and so on. But soon he realized, when the German-- when the Germans in Auschwitz took him over, that he's no longer what he used to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's no longer the boss.

speaker: interviewee
text: He is no longer the boss.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened to you and your family on your arrival at Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as I mentioned a while ago, the whole family was in this cattle car. I would say we were about more than about 80, 90 people packed in. There was no room even to stand. Forget about sitting down or lying down. Naturally, without any food and without any sanitary conditions.

speaker: interviewer
text: For three days.

speaker: interviewee
text: For three days. People were choking, people were-- just, it was impossible to imagine a-- a-- a transport like this. Finally, when you came to Auschwitz, and the doors opened up, we came off this-- this cattle car, and we tried to stay-- the family tried to stay together. The Germans were [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] telling us for the men to go separately and the women to go separately. And we were right away separated from each other. And the men-- again, as far as I know, the men were separated from those who were still looking well enough physically to be-- according to the estimation of the German officer who selected us-- that he is capable for work was placed in one area. And those who were not were placed to a different area. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: But-- excuse me for interrupting. First you were separated, the men from the women.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your mother and your two sisters--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't-- they were marched away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's the last time you saw them.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the last time I saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you were left with your father and your brother, is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I didn't mean to interrupt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then-- and then I was separated-- I-- my brother and I were separated from my father just from this group. Whatever happened to them, from him-- I mean, to him, I never found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the last time you saw your  father.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the last time.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Go-- go-- go-- go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then my brother and me, we were marched into a barrack. And before we-- before we came into that barrack, we went to-- we were marched off to a place in front of a-- some sort of barrack, a huge one. We were stripped from our clothing, shaved all over, and go in to take a bath. When we came out, everyone was handed some sort of a article of clothing-- a pair of pants, some sort of a jacket, a pair of shoes, sometimes two right shoes or two left shoes, a pair of shoes from two different colors, and so on-- just to be able to put on.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were not uniforms. They were just civilian--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just civilian-- civilian clothing. I assume it was taken from others who left them before we came in. And I suppose the same thing happened to our clothing, when-- when-- what we took off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were marched into a barrack, also packed. And we stayed in this place. We were there-- we didn't work. And I was there for about 18 days, until I was shipped out to work in a different-- to a labor camp in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the barrack for me. Could you describe the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The barrack?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a huge-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what it was. A wooden barrack, or it was-- I don't remember exactly if it was from brick or whatever. A cement floor. No beds. Nothing whatsoever. We-- and it was so packed that we didn't have room to lay down. We slept sitting down, with our legs crossed. And somebody else was sitting in front of me. And this is the way we sat, and this is the way we slept. The-- the food consisted of a slice of bread and two soups a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soup. Two soups a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Twice-- twice a day, you got soup.

speaker: interviewee
text: Twice a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And bread once.

speaker: interviewee
text: About a-- a slice about the thickness of a slice of bread that you would get today in the-- in the-- in a supermarket.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the sanitary facilities?

speaker: interviewee
text: The sanitary facility, there was, like, a bucket standing in the corner. And after a while, when it was filled, one of us had to take it out and dump it someplace and again bring it back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in this barrack for 18 days?

speaker: interviewee
text: For 18 days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened was that, during the day, since we didn't do anything, we-- we were allowed to go outside this barrack, just standing around. And, for some reason, my brother moved over to a different barrack, as far as I can-- as far as I could tell. And, since this bar-- excuse me. Since the Jews of this barrack were selected to be shipped out to work, he was among them. And the evening-- and I didn't realize it. But towards evening, when we went inside the barrack to be ready to go to sleep, I noticed that he is missing. Because he usually-- we would try to get together towards evening, so when we go in to sleep, we should stay together. In this case, he disappeared. And I realized that, since the barrack next to this one, to ours, was selected to go out to be shipped out to work, he must have been included in this group.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old-- how old was your brother at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in 1944. I was 20-- he was about 18 years old, 17 years old. The following day, I was looking around for a-- area, hoping that another barrack will be chosen to be shipped out. And it just happened that one of these barracks were emptied and marched over to the place where they would be shipped out. I joined this group. And they took off my clothes, and they gave me these striped uniform-- dressed-- like-- you know, with the rest of others. And I was shipped out to Germany, to a camp. The reason why I did it was that I was hoping that maybe, because I was shipped out the next day, then, from my brother, maybe by sheer coincidence I would be sent to the same place where he was, and we will be together again. But this never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They sent-- they sent me to a camp-- to an area by the name of Eule--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little-- a little town in-- in southern-- [INAUDIBLE] in the southern Silesia, in the German part on the-- part of Germany, close to the Polish border. And I was there for about three weeks. And then, they sent me to work. What

speaker: interviewer
text: Kind of-- what kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- the work consisted that a group-- the-- the group of peop-- of inmates that worked in this area consisted of a few groups. They-- we were split up in groups. One was chopping down trees. Another group would clean the-- the branches from the trees. And my-- my group, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we would carry these trees from one area-- from this area where it was-- where the-- from the area that they were cleaned to a-- to another place and drop them. And come back and pick up another one, and take them there. And we were about-- we were allowed about six, seven men to carry a tree. And after living under the most horrible conditions in the Lodz ghetto, starving for four years, freezing, being sick, swollen, and so on, you can imagine, we were skin and bones. And we had to carry on our shoulders these trees. Within after the first half hour, the skins were ripped off from our bones, here, from the shoulders. And, on top of it, we had to carry those trees. You can imagine how we felt about it. How agonizing and painful it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were not logs. These were the whole tree.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whole-- whole-- I mean-- the whole-- yeah, the whole log. I mean, it was not-- it was-- no, it was not cut up. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far did you carry them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a few hundred-- a few hundred meters. Quite-- quite a distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the road or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. We dropped them, and I think that they chopped them up over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- how-- how long did this work go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on, I would say, for about, uh-- October, Nov-- about almost three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was in the late fall.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the late, yes, in the late fall, and the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the winter. And then I couldn't-- I couldn't continue any longer to do this kind of work. They shipped me to a hospital camp. This was, I think, either in the beginning of '45 or in the very-- must have been either late in December of the-- of '44 or in the beginning of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the middle of the winter.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the-- oh yes, in the middle of the winter. I was shipped out to a hospital camp. I thought for sure that I will go to-- to Auschwitz, but it didn't matter anymore. I couldn't work. And they-- they shipped me out, because I couldn't work. But I went-- I sent-- I was sent to this camp in Dornhau. It was also not too far from this camp, also in the lower Silesia of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why-- why do you think that you were not sent back to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in those days, I didn't know what happened, what was the reason. But now I realize that in-- in-- in Ger-- in January, Auschwitz was liberated or evacuated. And they didn't take in any more inmates from the adjacent camps. That's why they sent me to this other camp. And evidently they didn't exterm-- exterminate me because they didn't have, I assume, any more facilities of exterminating. Because in the-- you see, all these-- all these informations came in later on, when we started to gather and to talk to people and to read about it and so on. Because in 19-- in 1945, they-- they stopped exterminating, because they didn't have the-- any more facilities. Most-- I mean, some of areas in western Germany was already liberated by the-- by the American army or the British army. Part of Poland, or most of Poland, was liberated by the Russian army. So that-- people were still dying by the thousands, by the tens of thousands. In my camp alone, hundreds of people died after the lib-- after the liberation, because they were so far gone that the-- the care that we got didn't help anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dornhau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dornhau.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the ca-- the camp where I was liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was this camp specifically for sick inmates?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This camp primarily was for sick inmates, except for one group that still did some work. And I would imagine, according to the work that I started to do eventually in this camp, that it was not for the sake of work, but it was for the sake of the German SS to guard us, and of the civilian few German men who supervised our work. Only to keep them occupied, or for them to have a reason to be occupied. Because around, I would say, in March of 1945, when the weather started to be warmer, and I realized that, by being in this camp, I will drop because of the conditions and the ration that we got-- because we only got a little bit of soup a day-- I decided, with the last of my ability, that I will have to go out of the camp and do some work in order to get an extra portion of soup. And when I was finally-- after being refused once-- I finally got the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the OK from the German who was-- from the-- from the doctor who was in charge of the camp. And he let me go out to work. The work that I did was actually ridiculous. Because there were two groups of us. We were digging holes in the ground. In those days, the-- the-- the ground was still frozen and we had to cut it with those picks. And we-- we made these holes, and another group came, and they filled it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we would advance to another area, a few hundred feet, to dig another hole. And when they got finished filling up the previous hole, they will come over to ours and fill up this one. So we would, among our-- the inmates, among themselves, would talk about it, and we would laugh-- to a certain degree-- that, what are we doing? Evidently, we are doing only in order for the-- for the SS who were watching us-- for them to have an excuse to have this kind of duty. Tell me, who was this camp doctor that--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Hungarian Jewish inmate. He was a-- an inmate, just like the rest of us. But since he was a doctor-- what he did, being a doctor, I don't know, because there was no way for him to do anything for us. First of all, as far as-- as far as being sick, everybody was sick. As far as being not capable to work, no one was able to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But his job was primarily to decide who--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Should work, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So if you didn't work, you starved slowly to death.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- I was laying-- oh, this happened a little later, when, the-- I was laying with people on my-- on my bunk. Because these-- we had bunks, there were about 10, 12 people laying on those-- on those bunks. But I would sit-- I would lay on these-- on this bunk with others, and we were talking to each other. And suddenly somebody would be quiet, and then we realized that-- that he-- he died. And we just didn't pay attention to them. We let them-- we let them-- we would let them lay there until a certain kommando among the inmates would come in and take him down and take him outside to-- to be dropped into some sort of a grave. Not a grave, but a ditch, to be filled up with-- with some sort of a chemical and covered with dirt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you mentioned that you left Auschwitz the day after your brother, hoping to be reunited.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- when you arrived at Eule, did you find your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never found him. And I was told by a friend of ours, from our hometown, who knew us well, that he was with my brother in a camp. And he was among the young group. And they did very light, or very easy work, for a short amount of time. And then the whole group was shipped out. They didn't know, at the time, that he was shipped to Auschwitz. But judging from the age of these young inmates, that they were shipped to Auschwitz because they were not capable of doing any work.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't know the name of the camp that--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I knew the-- I-- I-- he mentioned to me the name of it, but it was some sort of a remote, small camp that it didn't register and it didn't-- it didn't have any consequence for me to remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: You say he mentioned-- this was a mutual friend--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a mutual--

speaker: interviewer
text: --who had been in this camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, yes. But he was not returned to Auschwitz. No, because-- no, because he was older than my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- a-- a usual, a normal inmate, that he did work in the camp, that-- together with the others.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. But he told you that your brother was--

speaker: interviewee
text: That he saw my brother in this camp, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --sent back to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
